name: 112 W. Main St.
description: This building is known as 'The Brick Store of Silvester and Salamanâ€°' it was built in 1854 and completed in 1855 by Henry Silvester and Abraham Salaman. It is both the first and oldest original brick (and stone) building in Grass Valley and the second oldest brick building in Nevada County. There have only been three owners of this building, Henry Sylvester, Paul Wesley Michell, and Elton and Helen Williams. Only six businesses have occupied the store in 145 years, H. Silvester Grocery, H. J. Fuchs Grocery, C. W. Jenkin Variety Store, Tresize Brothers Stationery Store, Williams Brothers, later changed to Williams Stationery Store, and later became Make Local Habit.

name: 400 Broad Street
description: Text of plaque: It was at this location on the afternoon of June 5, 1880 that a fire erupted in John White's upholstery shop. More than fifty buildings - including the nearby New York Hotel and most of the Chinese Quarter - were destroyed within an hour, but from the ashes of that conflagration a new business district emerged and York Street was created to ensure better downtown fire protection.  Then, early in the 20th century, a doctor's office was established here. The longest-serving physician to occupy this corner was Bernard W. Hummelt, MD (1906 - 1998), who, between 1934 and his retirement in 1988, practiced internal medicine, orthopedics and general surgery. Over the course of his lengthy career he delivered over 1500 Nevada County babies. Dr. Hummelt was among the earliest physicians in the nation to administer penicillin and was instrumental in the founding of Sierra Nevada Memorial Hospital - where he served as its third Chief of Staff. Placed by the family of Bernard W. Hummelt, M.D. to honor the centennial of Dr. Hummelt's birth August 5,1906 A remarkable life- an exemplary career.

name: 545 Main Street
description: Druggist and County Treasurer Edward F. Spence built this house on 3 lots in 1866 and lived here with his wife Marietta until her death in 1868. He founded Spenceville, organized banks in San Jose and Southern California, served as Mayor of Los Angeles, and was instrumental in establishing the Mt. Wilson Observatory. County Judge Addison C. Niles bought the house from Spence in 1870 and occupied it with his wife Elizabeth until his election to the California Supreme Court in 1871. The City of Niles, California, is named for him. Niles Searls bought the house from his brother-in-law, Addison Niles, in 1871. When he became a Supreme Court Commissioner in 1885 he and his wife Mary gave the house to son Fred and his bride, Helen Pond, as a wedding present. In 1887 Niles Searls became Chief Justice of the California Supreme Court, and served on the Supreme Court Commission from 1893 to 1899. Attorney Fred Searls (a native of Nevada City) and his wife Helen (a native of Downieville) lived here from 1885 until his death in 1929. The last member of the family to reside here was Fred Searls III, great-grandson of Niles Searls.

name: Aaron Augustus Sargent Homestead
description: Homesteads of Nevada Countyâ€™s Statesman, a 49er. Arrived here In 1850 as a journeyman printer. Editor and owner of the countyÃ

name: Allen Chapman House
description: Allen Chapman House Built in 1856. The home and dental office of Allen Chapman, Nellie Chapman, and their son C. W. Chapman. Nellie Pooler Chapman was the first woman licensed to practice dentistry in the western United States.

name: Alpha and Omega Hydraulic
description: One mile north of here were the towns of Alpha and Omega, named by gold miners in the early 1850â€™s. The tremendous hydraulic diggings, visible from near this point, engulfed most of the original townsites. Alpha was the birthplace of famed opera singer Emma Nevada. Mining at Omega continued until 1949, and lumbering operations are carried on there today.

name: Alvin Stevens Trivelpiece
description: Alvin Stevens Trivelpiece 1903-1967. Californian, Clamper, Nevada County Historian, Newspaperman, Friend and a Founding Father of the Malakoff State Park, in whose memory these benches were placed.

name: American Hill Diggings
description: On this site in 1853 Edward E. Matteson developed one of the first hydraulic mines, an operation in which gold was washed from great quantities of earth with high pressure streams of water. The method used here became a standard of the mining industry through out the world.Second plaque dedicated 2006 by Native Sons of the Golden West: The Hydraulic Monitor, Pipe & Gate Valve displayed here was installed by Native Sons of the Golden West, Hydraulic Parlor 56, Nevada City, January 2006. Note: The first approach to hydraulic mining may have been that of Antoine Chabot, said to have used water to direct gravel into the sluicebox at Buckeye Hill in 1852. But Matteson fully developed the system in 1853 by attaching a nozzle to a rawhide hose to wash down soil for gold recovery at American Hill. By the 1860s Nevada County miners universally credited Matteson with the invention of hydraulic mining and other labor-saving devices.

name: Anthony House Site
description: Anthony House and Ranch. In 1849, a Mr. Anthony staked out 160 acres on the north side of Deer Creek on the road which would become part of the Marysville-Henness Pass-Virginia City trade route, and built a house and out-buildings. While further details about Mr. Anthony are unknown, the name Anthony House prevailed through a succession of owners. The property was up for sale in 1852 and purchased by Mr. S. P. French. By that time it was a flourishing stage stop, serving as a hotel, restaurant, livery stable and post office. The original house burned down in 1876 but was quickly rebuilt. Subsequent owners included William Henry Davey, Nevada County Supervisor 1908-1916 and later his son, Frank Davey. Stage stop and post office operations continued to 1906 when the post office was moved to Smartsville. Thereafter, declining traffic on the trade route led to farming and cattle raising as the main use of the property. By 1970 when Boise Cascade began development of this area, the property had fallen into disrepair and the house was burned down as a training exercise by the Penn Valley Fire Protection District. Construction of Anthony Dam here on Deer Creek submerged any remains of this historic site under Lake Wildwood.

name: Baruh House
description: The Baruh Home Built 1852. This site and home of two rooms was bought in 1866 by Mr. and Mrs. A. Baruh, grandparents of Mrs. Lucille Sherwood. Mrs. Clara Baruh Quigley, a charter member of Laurel Parlor No. 6, Native Daughters of the Golden West. Mr. Joseph Baruh, son, charter member of Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, Native Sons of the Golden West. Other family members: daughters Jenny Baruh Zellerbach, Rebecca Baruh Gabriel, sons Moses, Marcus and David. Mr. Aaron Baruh was a businessman operating a grocery store on Commercial Street, receiving gold dust for the commodities in the gold rush area. Later he operated a saloon and was very active in the early settlement of Nevada City. Note: Originally built by Jacob Coleman as a two-room cottage, it was expanded in 1866 by Aaron Baruh.

name: Bell Hill School Bell
description: The Bell Hill School Bell 1877. This school bell is lovingly dedicated to the children and teachers of the Grass Valley School District who daily answered its melodious tone. The bell was cast at the Buckeye Bell Foundry in Cincinnati by Vanduzen and Tift, and the rotary yoke was patented in 1868. The bell was placed in this beautiful monument by Harold J. Perry, a member of the Grass Valley Host Lions Club. Dedicated March 30,1985 as a part of the 100th Anniversary Celebration of Quartz Parlor No. 58.

name: Ben Taylor Home
description: Benjamin Franklin Taylor (1826-1913) arrived in Grass Valley on September 22, 1849, as part of a group of Mexican War veterans. He then established the Buena Vista Ranch, near the intersection of modern You Bet Road and Highway 174, where he engaged in farming and ranching. Prior to 1863, he bought a large piece of property in Grass Valley, on which in 1865-6 he built the home at what is now 652 Linden Avenue. Mr. Taylor was active in local politics and was part of the partnership that built a toll road between Grass Valley and Colfax, roughly following what is now Dog Bar Road. In 1868, he bought out some of his partners and became the principal owner. As of the time of its landmark registration, the home was still owned by Taylor's descendants.

name: Binkelmann Brewery
description: Born in Wurtemberg, Germany on January 2, 1828, David Binkelmann came to Grass Valley in 1853 after only a year as a new immigrant in New York. He mined for a time on Wolf Creek, then worked in a bakery. He became a renter of the business, but after two years sold the bakery operation to Herrman Maurer and bought a half-interest in a brewery with John Frank, operating in the rear of the Western Hotel which burned in 1856. The brick brewery was constructed in 1861, replacing a smaller 1856 frame building on North Auburn Street. Frank Richard bought out the partner in 1861 and was in turn bought out by Binkelmann shortly later. He ran the brewery until his death on May 23, 1894, leaving his wife Sophie to carry on until her death in 1915, when the brewery was bequeathed to her son, David J. Binkelmann. Note: The Brewery building, which has a beautiful vaulted brick cellar, has been incorporated into the hotel across the street.

name: Blue Tent
description: Blue Tent is a historic mining community established in 1850 and named after its first dwelling, a tent made of blue denim. In its heyday, it had a population of over 350, and its own store, dancehall, blacksmith shop, boarding house and other buildings.  A non-denominational Union Church was erected in 1861, and a school was established in 1868. A substantial Chinatown existed just north of  Blue Tent. The community was connected by stage to Nevada City, and by the world's first long distance telephone line established in 1878, to the mining communities around the San Juan Ridge. A post office existed between 1878 and 1889. In addition to mining, Blue Tent for many years had a thriving industry harvesting ice and shipping it throughout California. Like many communities that depended on hydraulic mining, Blue Tent began to decline following the 1884 Sawyer decision, forbidding the discharge of mining debris into the Yuba River. As mining declined, agriculture began to predominate. Today, the Blue Tent district is a pleasant, rural, largely residential area. Not much evidence remains of the mining district, except the Blue Tent school, which is still at the site to which it was moved in 1892. It has been converted to a private residence, but remains painted a vivid blue.

name: Boardman House
description: Notable as one of the earliest recorded houses in Nevada City and for its history of prominent owners. The tradition that the legendary William Morris Stewart built the house in 1853 cannot be documented for no deeds remain earlier than 1858. However, the house appears as the residence of P. G. Womack in the first map of the city prepared in 1855 and published in 1856. Other owners included the famous mining engineer, Capt. Thos. Mein, whose son William Wallace Mein was born in the house in 1873. The family of the late U.S. Representative Harry Englebright purchased the house in 1897 and resided there until 1959. The interior shows the work of many occupants and the exterior reflects eclectic borrowings from many past styles.

name: Boca Brewery
description: Boca. Noted as a railroad, ice and lumbering center since 1867 but most fondly remembered and hereby honored as home of the Boca Brewery which was located 1/2 mile south of this point across the Truckee River. Boca Lager Beer was world famous and mercifully slaked the thirst of a grateful mankind. Second plaque placed 2001 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus: Boca Beer. Warranted to keep in any weather.

name: Boca Dam - Newlands Reclamation Project
description: Designated a National Historic Landmark 1981: Boca Dam 1937-1940. United States Department of the Interior, Harold L. Ickes, Secretary; Bureau of Reclamation, John C. Page, Commissioner; R. F. Walter, Chief Engineer; L. J. Foster and C. S. Hale, Construction Engineers. Height 116 feet, Length 1629 feet, Storage capacity 40,901 acre-feet. Contractor, George W. Condon Co. Note: Originally called the Truckee-Carson Project, the Newlands Project covers a large area in western Nevada and a smaller area of Truckee-Tahoe area. One of five authorized by the Federal Reclamation Act of 1902, to reclaim arid lands for agricultural purposes. Renamed in 1919 in honor of U.S. Senator Francis G. Newlands of Nevada, water from natural flow and storage in Lake Tahoe and Boca Reservoir is diverted from Truckee River into the 32.5-mile Truckee Canal and then discharged into the Carson River.

name: Boca Ice Harvest Site
description: From 1868 to 1927 huge quantities of ice were harvested from the Truckee Basin. This provided employment and significantly contributed to the areaâ€™s economic growth and its colorful history. Boca Pond was one of the first to be used to harvest ice and ship it across the nation. It was one of the last of the ice plants to survive competition from artificial ice companies.

name: Bridgeport Covered Bridge
description: This covered bridge across the South Fork of the Yuba River at Bridgeport, Nevada County. Built in 1862 by David Isaac John Wood with lumber from his mill in Sierra County, this bridge was part of the Virginia Turnpike Company Toll Road which served the Northern Mines and the busy Nevada Comstock Lode. Utilizing a combination truss and arch construction, it is one of the oldest housed spans in the West and the longest single span, wooden covered bridge in the United States.The plaque is dedicated to those pioneer Americans who came to California in search of a new life and passed this way to establish their homes in the Northern Mine country. Note: The second plaque is full of errors. Bridgeport originally was known as "Nye's Landing" because Urias and Manuel Nye built a trading post here in 1849, but "Nye's Crossing" was on the Middle Fork of the Yuba River, near the mouth of Oregon Creek. Furthermore, the builder's name was David Ingerfield John Wood.

name: Brimskill Building
description: Built by Edward Brimskill, 1849-1851. Rear section built 1849. Iron shutters a protection from fire and robbers, imported from Scotland. Used in early days as general store and miners supply. Wells Fargo maintained desk room here for many years and shipped thousands of dollars of gold from here. One of the best preserved buildings in California of the gold rush era. Said to be oldest building in Nevada County.

name: Calanan Park
description: Text of plaque: Native Daughters Garden in George Calanan Park. In 1922, Laurel Parlor No. 6, Native Daughters of the Golden West acquired two small lots to create a memorial park for the veterans and those who died in World War I. In the interest of 'a valuable public improvement,' Laurel Parlor sold the lot to the U.S. Forest Service for a district office in 1936. The advent of World War II redirected federal resources and the planned 6,000 square foot building was never constructed. The lot remained a community park. In 1967, the park was dedicated to honor George Humphrey Calanan (1872- 1951), a much-loved Town Trustee (City Councilman), health officer, assessor, volunteer fireman, and long time City Clerk. He was known as 'the man who got things done.' The park was granted to the City of Nevada City by the United States Department of the Interior on December 1, 1971.

name: Calanan Park Drill Core
description: Shaft Drill Core 1935-1936. Experimental vertical shaft, 5' in diameter to 1125' depth thru serpentine and gabbro at Idaho-Maryland Gold Mine, Grass Valley. Machinery invented by J. B. Newsom. Greatest drilled footage, 21' in three days. Core weight 11-tons. Donated to Nevada City by Kenneth E. Baker.

name: Calanan Park Monitor
description: The first hydraulic mining originated on American Hill near Nevada City, California in 1853. This minerâ€™s giant known as the monitor washed hillsides away under high pressure uncovering gold bearing gravel beds. This mining technique flourished profitably until 1884 when the Sawyer decision closed hydraulic mining. Rivers were being filled with mud and debris causing flooding of the rich farmlands below.

name: Canyon Creek Bridge Site
description: The Canyon Creek Bridge was built in 1895 by Cotton Brothers and Co. of Oakland, California, for $1,725. Completed two years earlier than the Old Auburn Bridge (No. 13,), it is of similar construction and design. Underneath it is the reservoir that supplies water to the residents of the town of Washington. Canyon Creek is a major tributary of the South Yuba River, and the scenic view of the rapids and falls above the bridge is outstanding. Note: After determining the bridge had outlived its useful life, the County erected a modern bridge in 2017 and placed this plaque: Site of a steel pony truss bridge erected in 1895 at Canyon Creek Crossing on the road linking Washington to the town of Maybert. There the Yuba Mine employed 13 Chinese and 27 Americans, one of the few mines still employing Chinese. In the 1880â€™s and 1890â€™s Maybert had a Post Office, store, boarding house, school and saloon. Fire destroyed all 20 of its dwellings in 1909. The old bridge was disassembled and is awaiting relocation by Nevada City as part of its hiking trails expansion.

name: Caroline Mead Hanson House
description: Homesite of Caroline Mead Hanson. Whose idea sparked the annual Donation Day sponsored by Grass Valley Ladies Relief Society since 1883. Note: Home of the originator of Grass Valley's Donation Day, that began In 1883. Mrs. Hansen, an invalid, sat in the bay window of her home and watched the children troop to school. Hearing of the Ladies Relief SocietyÃ

name: Charles Marsh House
description: Built about 1866 by Charles Marsh, who arrived here in July, 1849, in what would later become Nevada City. Marsh was the first County Surveyor and the first County Supervisor from Nevada City. He created the water distribution system which formed the basis for P.G.& E. and the Nevada Irrigation District. Marsh was influential in the routing of the Central Pacific Railroad and served on its original Board of Directors, as well as the Board of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. He was also instrumental in creating a Library Association in Nevada City.

name: Cherokee
description: Cherokee is a former gold mining community In Nevada County, located on the San Juan Ridge about 4 miles east of North San Juan. At the time of the Gold Rush, this area was occupied by the Nisenan. The Gold Rush came to Cherokee in 1850, when a group of Cherokee Indians began mining there and gave it its name. The town's heyday was between 1856 and 1865. It had two hotels, the Grizzly and Curnowâ€™s, saloons, several stores, a butcher, a shoe shop, a resident physician and drugstore, and a blacksmith. Its sawmill had a capacity of 10,000 ft. of lumber a day. The local newspaper was the Cherokee Journal. There was stagecoach service to Nevada City. After 1878, Cherokee was connected to the worldâ€™s first long distance telephone line which connected the mining communities along the San Juan Ridge from French Corral to the Bowman Lake area. The town began to decline in the 1880â€™s, after hydraulic mining was restricted by the Sawyer decision. By 1924, the town was reduced to "one store; and the people live principally by farming, very little active mining being carried on here." Today, all that remains of Cherokee is the schoolhouse located on Sages Road just north of Tyler Foote Crossing Road, the historic cemetery, and a lot of land scarred by hydraulic mining.

name: Chicago Park Historic Agricultural Community
description:
The Chicago Park Historic Agricultural Community occupies roughly the area on either side of Highway 174 between the Bear River and Brunswick Road. It is centered on the villages of Chicago Park and Peardale. Prior to the 1840s, this area was populated by the Nisenan. In August 1849, what is reported to have been Nevada County's second store was erected on land overlooking the junction of the Bear River and Greenhorn Creek. In the 1850s, local residents led by Simmon Storms succeeded in having the Nisenan removed to a reservation near Tehama. During that period, Benjamin Taylor settled on a ranch in the area which he named Buena Vista, after the Mexican War battle in which he had participated, and engaged in farming and horse breeding. Others who settled in the area found it very suitable for growing fruit, especially pears.
In 1872, the Coleman Brothers purchased the Buena Vista and Storms ranches, and other properties in the area, as they were developing the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. Completed in 1876, the Railroad spurred the agricultural development of the area. In 1887, a group of Colfax and Chicago, Illinois developers acquired several thousand acres of land and drew up plans for the Chicago Park Colony. The Colony was to consist of 20 and 40 acre tracts as well as town lots laid out around a public square with streets named for prominent streets in Chicago. Much of the planned development never materialized, but reportedly almost 7000 acres were sold, principally to residents of Chicago. Many of the tracts were planted with orchards, and the area was referred to as the Bartlett Pear Belt. The railroad station at Storms Station was renamed Chicago Park Station, There were also stations at Buena Vista and Peardale. Fruit was king in the area for about 100-years. Orchards growing pears, plums, peaches and apples were everywhere. Because water was not abundant, dry farming techniques were mastered which improved fruit color. In 1915, local Bartlett pears were honored at the Pacific International Exposition in San Francisco. As the 20th century progressed, pear blight appeared, causing some growers to switch to other crops.

name: Chief Truckee
description: Chief Truckee. The legendary Paiute leader; friend and guide to pioneers; breveted a 'Captain' by John C. Fremont; gave his name to this valley, river and town; died near Dayton, Nevada, in 1860.

name: China Wall
description: Third plaque: The China Wall. In the 1850s, Chinese laborers, who were an essential part of the community, built the wall you see before you. Large natural stones were hand stacked for its construction. The wall extends 5 miles in either direction, however much of it has disintegrated over the years. This lot, along with the lot across the Gephardt's Union Turnpike, and the adjoining lots, are referred to in the Nevada County tax records as the Ã¯China LotsÃ

name: China Wall at Donner Summit
description: Charles Crocker, construction chief of the Central Pacific Railroad (CPRR), contracted for a workforce of approximately 12,000 Chinese laborers to push the CPRR tracks over its Trans-Sierra crossing on its race east to meet with the Union Pacific at Promontory, Utah Territory. A railroad retaining wall and fill, constructed of Sierra granite, stand silently above on the pass as a lasting monument to these Asian 'Master Builders' who left an indelible mark on the history of California and the West.

name: Chinatown Grass Valley (Site)
description: Chinatown, Grass Valley 1850-1938. Of the many gold rush Immigrants the Chinese were noted for their honest, sober and industrious characteristics. Each mining camp had its Chinatown and Grass Valley was second only to San FranciscoÃ

name: Chinese Cemetary
description: The 1852 state census recorded 3,396 Chinese living in Nevada County, about 20% of its population. Soon, anti-Chinese sentiment forced the Chinese out of the cemeteries managed by white people and into their own segregated cemetery. By the 1870s they began burial at the site of the present Chinese Cemetery. The inscription on the shrine inside the Cemetery states that the Cemetery was "reconstructed" on August 6, 1891, a date that also appears on the original gate at the entrance of the Cemetery. The site is owned presently by the Thiem family who operate the Inn Town Campground which surrounds the Cemetery, with the Chinese Historical Society having a historic preservation easement.

name: Chris Stevens Court
description: tevens Court honors John Christopher Stevens, United States Ambassador to Libya, who died in the service of his country September 11, 2012, in Benghazi. 'Chris' Stevens was born in Nevada County, the offspring of three generations of Grass Valleyans. Chris loved Grass Valley and spent many happy hours there. His fascination in foreign affairs and history led him to a Peace Corps stint in Morocco, followed by work in international legal law practice in San Francisco and Washington D.C. He then joined the Foreign Service, in which he served in Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Tunisia, Jerusalem and Syria. When revolution broke out in Libya, Chris was sent out on a rusty Greek freighter to report and make recommendations for U.S. policy. His work led to appointment as U.S. Ambassador to the fledgling nation. Early on he established rapport with the Libyans, as he had with the people of the Middle East and Africa wherever he was stationed with the Foreign Service. Rather than hiding behind fortified walls, he strolled the streets, talking to the people. One day after the attack that cost him his life, thousands of Libyans took to the streets protesting his death. They carried hand printed signs, some reading: 'Chris Stevens was a friend to all Libyans'.

name: Chuck Yeager Monument
description: A series of 5 plaques chronicles the career of Gen. Charles E. "Chuck" Yeager, a distinguished aviator, war hero and test pilot, who retired to this area. An F-104 Starfighter, the world's first Mach 2 operational aircraft which Yeager tested in 1954, is strikingly displayed behind the plaques.

name: Clinton Narrow Gauge Railroad
description: First steam railroad logging operation in the Truckee River Basin. Commenced operation in the late 1870â€™s under the supervision of Fred Burckhalter, head of the Pacific Lumber and Wood Co. Line extended 10 miles along Juniper Creek with hope of eventual service to Lake Tahoe. Line abandoned in 1901 after several conflagrations at the PL&W Co. mill in Clinton.

name: Colley House
description: Queen Anne Style Built in 1860 Home of Ida Colley and niece, Winifred Mulloy, founders of Laurel Parlor #6 Native Daughters of the Golden West. Now Deer Creek Inn Bed & Breakfast.

name: Columbia Hill School
description: The schoolhouse, built in 1875, was still in use as a school in the 1970's. In 1980 the State of California condemned the schoolhouse as unsafe. It was remodeled and used for a while as a community center. Later a new community center was established next door, and the old school building was used for other purposes. It is now known as the North Columbia Schoolhouse Cultural Center. The original Columbia Hill School was built prior to March 11,1867.

name: Commercial Row / Brickelltown Historic District
description: The Commercial Row/ Brickelltown Historic District lies in the center of Truckee along the tracks of the first transcontinental railroad. It includes the first built areas of Truckee. Because of fires, most of the buildings in the district range from 1870 to the 1920s. The district illustrates Truckee's relationship with the railroad and the lumber industry and Truckee's appearance more than 100-years ago. Truckee was originally called Coburn Station, and then renamed around 1868 after a Paiute chief. The District was dedicated in 2009 by the Truckee Town Council.

name: Crabtree House
description: Lotta Crabtree was born in New York in 1847. In 1852 the gold fever brought Mr. Crabtree to California, and a year later his wife and Lotta followed. Several months after arriving in San Francisco Mrs. Crabtree and Lotta went to Grass Valley and started a boarding house for miners with Mr. Crabtree. It was here that Lotta met Lola Montez, who taught her to sing and dance. Lotta made her first public appearance in Plumas County, which led to a successful career on stage, both here and abroad.

name: Davis Mill
description: The Davis Mill is a well preserved stamp mill which served a small gold mine run by the Davis family from 1915 to 1940.

name: Donner Camp at Alder Creek Valley
description:
This site has three plaques and was designated a National Historical Landmark in 1966. 

First plaque dedicated 1996 by Tahoe National Forest: In the fall of 1846, 25 members of the Donner Party became trapped by an early snowstorm here at Alder Creek Valley. The George and Jacob Donner families, their teamsters, and fellow travelers suffered extreme hardship and starvation. They spent the winter here cut off from the rest of their party who camped at Donner Lake. Only eleven survived the ordeal. Their survival, against desperate odds, stands as a testament to the enduring pioneer spirit that helped shape the future of California.

Second plaque dedicated 1996 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus: They gave unselfishly, their fortunes and their lives that their children should survive. Near this site, in the winter of 1846, two pioneer women gave up their lives for their families. Tamsen and Elizabeth Donner feared their many children could not survive the ravages of cold and starvation when the party was caught in an early blizzard. They provided care and comfort to their families and companions throughout the snowbound winter, desperately trying to prevent the death of their loved ones. Both lost their lives. However, most of their children survived to carry their mothers' dreams of new life and new beginnings to the valleys of California. The summit that they never crossed now bears the family name. An inspiration to all who followed their footsteps across the Sierra Nevada Mountains, we herein honor the memory and the sacrifices of these women. Their struggle to survive, enduring hardships we can barely imagine, remains a legacy of the pioneer spirit. On this 150th anniversary of their tragic encampment the Chief Truckee Chapter of E Clampus Vitus dedicates this monument to their spirit and the preservation of their memory that we not forget the sacrifices they made in opening California to its destiny.

The Donner Camp Site plaque is located in the middle of the Donner Camp Interpretive Trail, just past the end of the boardwalk.

Second plaque dedicated 1960 by Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus, Historical Society, Tahoe National Forest USFS, Truckee-Donner Chamber of Commerce, and Native Daughters of the Golden West: On October 28, 1846 the six covered wagons brought west by George and Jacob Donner and their families halted here for repairs. By March of 1847 one half of the party of 22 adults and children had died of starvation and cold. They came west seeking a new life and found misery and death.

name: Donner Emigrant Trail
description: The Emigrant Trail in the Pioneer Days of California came through the low pass to the north, facing this monument. The Trail turned west at this point for a distance of twenty-six hundred feet where a tablet describes the route then followed. Adjoining plaque dedicated 2014: First Permanent Emigrant Trail Markers. The nearby monument was envisioned in 1929 by C.F. McGlashan, Truckeeâ€™s foremost resident 1872-1931 and author of the 1880 classic, The History of the Donner Party; P.M. Weddell, who placed wooden signs on the Donner Trail from Verdi, Nevada over Coldstream Pass to Lake Mary from 1924 until his death in 1951; and W.F. Knowland, U. S. Senator (1945-1959) and the chairman of The Native Sons of the Golden Westâ€™s Landmark Commission. The three trail authorities met in McGlashanâ€™s law office in Truckee to draw up the text for the plaque on this monument, listed as #3 on the 1930 Weddell map to the right and a companion plaque for the monument listed as #5 located one half mile to the west on old Hwy 40 and Coldstream Road. Note: According to the P. M. Weddell map of the Historic Emigrant Road, this resting place of the Native Sons' marker is close to its original location.

name: Donner Memorial State Park
description: The Origin Of The Park. Joseph Marzen sold one acre of land east of Donner Lake to Charles F. McGlashan for the purpose of erecting a monument to the Donner Party. McGlashan then sold the land to the Native Sons of the Golden West (NSGW) for $1.00 in August of 1909. At the center of the acre, according to McGlashan, was the site of the Breen Cabin, where the monument would be situated. On June 6, 1918, the NSGW dedicated the completed Pioneer Memorial. California Governor William D. Stephens signed legislation acquiring 'Donner Monument and lands contingent thereto.' On May 23, 1928, the Grand Parlor of the Native Sons of the Golden West conveyed eleven acres of land to the People of the State of California. The deed for the parcel adjacent to the Lincoln Highway included one provision: 'The tract of land above described shall be known and designated as the Donner State Monument and shall be a memorial to the California Pioneers of the Overland Trailâ€™. Note: The plaque is inside the building, to the left as you enter.

name: Donner or Pioneer Monument
description: Monument dedicated and first two plaques placed 1918 by the Governors of California and Nevada, three survivors of the Donner Party, and Native Daughters of the Golden West. First plaque: Virile to risk and find, Kindly withal and a ready help. Facing the brunt of fate. Indomitable-unafraid." Second plaque: In commemoration of the pioneers who crossed the plains to settle in California. Landmark registered and third plaque placed 1962 by State of California, CHL 134: Donner Party. Near this spot stood the Breen Cabin of the party of emigrants who started for California from Springfield, Illinois, in April, 1846, under the leadership of Captain George Donner. Delays occurred and when the party reached this locality, on October 29, the Truckee Pass emigrant road was concealed by snow. The height of the shaft of the monument indicates the depth of the snow, which was twenty-two feet. After futile efforts to cross the summit the party was compelled to encamp for the winter. The Graves cabin was situated about three- quarters of a mile to the eastward, the Murphy cabin was about two hundred yards southwest of the monument, and the Donner tents were at the head of Alder Creek. Ninety people were in the party and forty-two perished, most of them from starvation and exposure.

name: Donner Party - Graves Cabin Site
description: Plaque dedicated 1919 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Donner Party 1846-1847. On this spot stood the Graves Cabin.

name: Donner Summit Bridge
description: First plaque dedicated 1926 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Dedicated to the pioneers who blazed the Overland Trail through these mountains. Built by the U. S. Bureau of Public Roads and the California Highway Commission, 1925-1926. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1998 by Nevada County: 1926 Donner Summit Bridge Restored 1995. Note: In 1926 it was anticipated that the bridge (built at a cost of about $40,000) would become a popular stopping place on the "Victory Highway." The bridge was designed by Norman C. Raab, whose wife, Ruby Bishop Raab, was the great-granddaughter of Isaac Hitchcock of the Stephens-Townsend-Murphy party. The original bridge arch was 110 feet long and 70 feet high, built on a compound curve, with a seven percent grade. Among those who attended the 1926 ceremony were Charles F. McGlashan and these descendants of Donner party survivors: James Fraser Lewis, Martha Jane Lewis, Susan A. Lewis, and Ernest H. Murphy. Norman Raab also designed the world-famous Bixby Creek Bridge on State Highway 1 at Big Sur, built in 1930-1931.

name: Donner Summit Tunnel
description: "Great Summit Tunnel of the Sierra Nevada. Beneath this plaque the first Transcontinental Railroad traversed the mighty Sierra Nevada range. The 1659 foot Long summit tunnel took over 15 months of Chinese muscle and sweat to build. The Chinese painstakingly hand drilled, then blasted the granite rock with black powder and newly invented nitroglycerine. A vertical shaft took 85 days to complete and allowed tunnel construction to proceed from the center as well as from the portals. The most difficult obstacle facing the Central Pacific Railroad was overcome when the tunnel was completed in August 1867. The first passenger train passed through the tunnel on June 18, 1868. The last train went through in 1993."

name: Doris Foley Library of Historical Research
description: Nevada City Public Library. Library Association formed December 19, 1857. First library was in Kidd and Knox Building, 228 Broad Street. It was moved to Oustamah Lodge IOOF No. 16 June 3, 1874. Nevada City Trustees assumed responsibilities October 1, 1902 and was moved into the Corcoran Building next to the Nevada Theater. The Carnegie Endowment was given to the city in 1907. The present library was dedicated October 4, 1907. City groups raised funds for furnishings and beautified grounds 1911. Note: In 1996 the Nevada City Council, the Nevada County Board of Supervisors, and the Nevada County Historical Landmarks Commission voted to rename this library for Doris Foley, an outstanding historian and author who contributed greatly to scholarly research in this county. In addition to her writings, she helped found the Nevada County Historical Society and was the first librarian at Searls Historical Library. The Doris Foley Library of Historical Research was formally dedicated on May 17,1997.

name: Dow Alexander Park
description: Formerly known as the City Square, this park was established by the Grass Valley Women's Improvement Club in 1911, as a place of rest for patrons of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. It was dedicated to the pioneers and veterans of the Spanish American War. Second plaque: Last on duty at Fort Point on San Francisco Bay. Grass Valley Womenâ€™s Improvement Club arranged with the US War Department for donation to be placed in City Square, now Dow Alexander Park. Transferred from Benicia Arsenal to here in 1911. Only known example of its type to exist. Weight: 9100 pounds.

name: Dr. W. C. Jones Home Site
description: Dr. W. C. Jones came to Grass Valley in 1874 after serving as physician and surgeon for the Central Pacific Railroad Co. during construction of the railroad between Colfax and Reno. He had three sons, Will, John, and Carl, who along with their father served the community as doctors until the last son died in 1944. In 1904 Dr. John Jones opened the first private hospital in Nevada County-the W. C. Jones Memorial Hospital, Grass Valley. A fourth son, George, became Superior Court Judge of Nevada County, serving from 1908 until 1925 and again from 1938 until he retired in 1947. Note: The city removed the historic house to make room for the police department.

name: Edward Coleman House
description: The Edward Coleman House. Edward Coleman built this house in 1866. While residing here, Coleman was President/Superintendent of the Idaho Mine, Director of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad, and had interests in the Mohawk Lumber Company. Coleman sold this house in 1893.

name: Edwards Crossing Bridge
description: Built in 1904 by the American Bridge Company of steel-connection-pin design to replace a wooden structure built in 1862 by William Edwards. In 1854 Edwards began operations here with a ferry, and in 1855 he built a bridge and toll road between Blue Tent and Lake City. His first bridge washed away in December 1861, and was replaced in the following year. The county acquired the bridge sometime after 1880. Note: Both this and the similar Gault bridge at Nevada City (No. 99) were built under the supervision of Nevada County Surveyor W. W. Waggoner.

name: Elk's Lodge
description: "After constructing a second–story meeting hall above two existing 19th century commercial buildings, the Nevada City Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks #518 dedicated its first lodge at this location on June 14, 1913. The lodge hall and street–level storefronts served the community until a devastating fire broke out on the morning of March 20, 2002. Property owners Ken and Kay Baker and Gary and Patty Tintle then initiated a reconstruction program that has resulted in the current building – faithful to the architecture and style of the historic structure it replaced."

name: Ellen Sargent
description: "Ellen Clark Sargent, a native of Massachusetts, arrived in Nevada City in 1852. A leader in the fight for women's right to vote, she founded the Nevada County Woman's Suffrage Association in 1869. She was the President of the California Woman Suffrage Association and Treasurer of the National Woman Suffrage Association. Her tireless work resulted in California women winning the right to vote in 1911. Her campaign for national women's suffrage led to the introduction in 1878 of the 19th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution by her husband, U.S. Senator A. A. Sargent. After many hard-fought battles by the Women's Suffrage Movement, the 19th Amendment was finally enacted into law on August 26, 1920."

name: Emigrant Trail on Coldstream Road
description: The Emigrant Trail in the pioneer days of California turned to the south at this point for approximately three miles, then went across the summit of the Sierras about a mile south of the present railroad. It was here that the Donner Party missed the trail owing to the early snows, resulting in the tragedy.

name: Emigrant Trail to Nevada City
description: In 1850, the year after the fabulous gold strike on Deer Creek in Nevada City, the Overland Emigrant Trail branched off the original Bear River route at this point on its way to Nevada City and the Sacramento Valley. Soon thereafter the trail became a toll road and later a public road. The deep vehicle ruts can still be seen as they cross and recross Highway 20 many times.

name: Emmanuel Episcopal Church
description: Historic Emmanuel Episcopal Church. Oldest Episcopal edifice in California. First services April 23, 1854 by Rt. Rev. William I. Kip, Bishop of Calif. This building opened for worship Aug. 1856. Note: The church was declared eligible for National Landmark status in 1982, but the vestry declined to have the church registered. The first services mentioned above were held in "a public hall" according to an account by Bishop Kip. On December 11, 1856, the Gold Hill Mining Company donated land to the parish that was bounded by Church, Walsh and Mill streets. The present church opened for worship in 1858. It is the oldest existing Episcopal Church in continuous use in California.

name: Empire House
description: This site at the lower end of Boston Ravine on the old main road from Grass Valley to Auburn has been the location of a public house since 1851. Charles Behrisch was the owner from 1851 to 1885. Ownership changed hands many times through the years. Purchased by Dominic Pajnich on August 1,1933, and was burned in 1934. Replaced with a 14-room hotel, named Empire Inn. Upstairs closed off in 1964, but remains a popular food and beverage stop today. Boston Ravine, established in 1849, was the earliest mining community in the area which subsequently grew into Grass Valley.

name: Empire Mine
description: This plaque marks the site of the Empire Mine, noted for its continuous operation 1850-1957 producing over $120,000,000 in gold.

name: Englebright House
description: Notable as one of the earliest recorded houses in Nevada City and for its history of prominent owners. The tradition that the legendary William Morris Stewart built the house in 1853 cannot be documented for no deeds remain earlier than 1858. However, the house appears as the residence of P. G. Womack in the first map of the city prepared in 1855 and published in 1856. Other owners included the famous mining engineer, Capt. Thos. Mein, whose son William Wallace Mein was born in the house in 1873. The family of the late U.S. Representative Harry Englebright purchased the house in 1897 and resided there until 1959. The interior shows the work of many occupants and the exterior reflects eclectic borrowings from many past styles.

name: English and New Salmon Mine Stamp Mill
description: This stamp mill was manufactured at the Miners Foundry and Supply Co., Nevada City, California, in the late 1800â€™s. After a circuitous journey, it has returned to its place of origin. It started its industrial life of crushing hard rock at the English Mine, and then the New Salmon Mine, owned by Donald McPherson, at Badger Hill. The stamp mill ceased operation in 1942 when all non-critical gold mines were shut down. After sitting idle through the years overgrown by trees and brush the stamp mill was moved in the 1970â€™s to the Nevada City home of King McPherson, who generously donated the stamp mill back to the Miners Foundry in the 1980â€™s. The stamp mill sat in pieces around the foundry, despite several attempts to reconstruct it. Through the efforts of the members of Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, Native Sons of the Golden West under the supervision of Mike Nevius, restoration was completed in the spring of 2000.

name: Ernie Bierwagen
description: Ernie was born February 1915 and died in February 2004, in Chicago Park, California. He was close to the land and the people he met along the way. He was a timeless leader in the agricultural community, his church, and was a friend to many. Always genuine, he was a man of strong faith that sustained him and those around him. Ernie and his family devoted their lives to making this world a better place. The love of the community by the Bierwagen Family continues to this day. Ernie often brought his family to observe the construction of this beautiful Reservoir. He knew the importance of water to nature, the food supply and the lives of all people. Ernie served 25 years as a Nevada Irrigation District Division II Director. With passion, he strived to protect and enhance the water supply. To all that visit this beautiful area, please enjoy.

name: Felix Gillet
description: Here lived and worked Felix Gillet, A Frenchman by birth, an American by adoption from 1871 to 1908 through whose vision and perseverance the horticulture of the world was enriched by the propagation and culture of walnuts, filberts, chestnuts, prunes. In commemoration of his work this tablet was set March 7, 1933 by the WomenÃ

name: Fippin's Blacksmith Shop
description: Republic of Rough and Ready April 1850 - July 1850. This community of 3000 people seceded from the Union to declare a free and independent state. Colonel E. F. Brundage was elected president. Congressional action on California statehood was pending at the time. Built in the mid 1850s by John Fippin and John Single. Fippin married Single's daughter Julia in 1867, and they raised 10 children. Local legend has it that one of Lotta Crabtree's earliest performances was atop the anvil here. The building today is a combination of the original blacksmith shop and Single's carpenter shop. W. H. (Bill) Fippin and Frank A. Fippin followed their father as smiths and ran the business until well into the 20th century. Members of the Fippin and Single families have served Rough and Ready for generations as County officials, School Trustees and Postmasters.

name: First Brick Building in Nevada City
description: Built in 1853. First brick building erected in this city. Dedicated to the memory of the pioneers of Nevada City. Note: This originally was the Dickerman carpenter shop. Bricks for this building were manufactured at Zeno P. Davis's brickyard on Lower Grass Valley Rd. in Nevada City (now called Searls Ave).

name: First Commercial Winery/305 Broad St.
description: "In the 1850s, over 100,000 grapevines were planted in Nevada County, often by French settlers on the western part of the San Juan Ridge. For some years, Augustine Isoard operated a wine and liquor store in this building. On June 1, 1860, he advertised in the Nevada Journal that "I have on hand a few gallons of Wine manufactured from the Grape by myself, in this city." This is believed to be the first commercial winery operation in Nevada County. After a lull in the middle of the 20th century, commercial grape growing and winemaking came roaring back. Nevada County became part of the Sierra Foothills American Viticultural Area in 1987 and home to many wineries and hundreds of acres of grapes."

name: First Long Distance Telephone
description: Built in 1877, the Ridge Telephone Co., connected French Corral with French Lake 58 miles away. It was operated by the Milton Mining Co., from this building which was built about 1853. This plaque donated by Sadie Winn Brainard. Note: This plaque was originally placed on the brick building in which the telephone office was located. After the building was razed in 1955 it was placed on a concrete monument on the shoulder of the present Pleasant Valley Road in front of a neighboring residence. The 1877 date is an error. The line began operation in 1878. The claim of being the "world's first" is still being debated. However, its challengers did not have the track record of the Ridge Telephone Company, which operated successfully into the 20th century over a much longer distance.

name: First Motorcycle Across Sierra
description: "George Adams Wyman. The first person to cross America by motorcycle, riding from San Francisco to New York City. May 16–July 6, 1903. George A. Wyman was the first person to make a transcontinental Journey across America by means of a motorized vehicle. Wyman rode a 200cc, 1.25-horsepower 1902 'California' motor-bicycle manufactured by the California Motor Company. On May 20th, Wyman departed the Summit Hotel at Donner Pass. He followed the railroad tracks through the snow sheds to intercept the road near the head of Donner Lake. He continued another 10 miles to Truckee, where he asked for directions and was urged to take the Prosser House road to the Boca siding. At Boca, he switched back to the tracks, passing through Verdi on to Reno, where he stopped for the night."

name: First Transcontinental Railroad--Truckee
description: While construction on Sierra tunnels delayed Central Pacific, advance forces at Truckee began building forty miles of track east and west of Truckee, moving all supplies by wagon and sled. Summit Tunnel was opened in December, 1867. The line reached Truckee April 3, 1868, and the Sierra was conquered. Rails reached Reno June 19, and construction advanced eastward one mile daily toward the meeting with Union Pacific at Promontory May 10,1869, to complete the first transcontinental railroad.

name: First Wagons Across the Sierra
description: The Stephens-Townsend-Murphy Party, with guidance from old Caleb Greenwood and Chief Truckee, brought their wagons up the Truckee River bed and on Nov. 25,1844, 191 days out of Council Bluffs, Iowa, passed this point and crossed the mighty Sierra summit directly to the west, blazing the famous California Emigrant Trail for all who followed.

name: Five-Stamp Mill 1893
description: Used to crush gold ore at the Fortuna Mine near Nevada City. Ore fed under iron 'stamps' from rear hopper. Steam, water or electric belt-driven power turned wheel which revolved cams. Donated to Nevada City by Bill Magers.

name: Floriston (Bronco Site)
description: Established in 1872 as Bronco, this railroad wood stop became a noted ice and lumber center and was renamed in 1891. The Floriston Pulp and Paper Company built the present town after 1899 and operated one of the worldÃ

name: Foote's Crossing Road
description: Footeâ€™s Crossing Road was built by A. D. Foote in 1913 as an all-season short-cut from Columbia Hill to the mines of the Alleghany area. Foote was president of the Tightner Mine Co., the largest gold producer in the region at that time. The road, a 15-foot shelf hanging on the sheer canyon wall above the Middle Fork of the Yuba River, is a spectacular engineering feat and scenic wonder. The rock walls, built without mortar are still intact today. Unforeseen difficulties in building the road caused it to far exceed specifications so Foote personally financed 60% of the total cost, the balance being paid by Nevada and Sierra counties and the U.S. Forest Service.

name: Gaston
description: A historic mining community. Placer mining around Gaston began in the early 1850â€™s. The first hard rock mine, the California Mine, was established in 1856. An ample supply of water was ensured by the arrival of the North Bloomfield ditch in 1870. In 1898, the mine, now known as the Gaston Mine, was acquired by the San Francisco based Gaston Gold Mining Company, which substantially upgraded operations. A town blossomed around the mine. The population was recorded as about 200 in 1900. There was stage service to the town of Washington, and to Nevada City. By 1904, the town boasted two stores, a hotel, a saloon, a hardware store, a Post Office, a water system, electric lights, a telephone line to Nevada City, a fire company and a school. The mine closed during World War I, because of a shortage of labor and supplies needed for the war effort. There was intermittent mining after the war. By the 1940sâ€™, the last remaining building was in shambles. Today, nothing readily visible remains of Gaston.

name: Gateway Cabin
description: This cabin was one of several built by Jack Wolert for Dick Joseph in 1939 on US Route 40. The cabin was constructed of lumber salvaged from houses being dismantled five miles north of Truckee at Hobart Mills and served as the Gateway Motelâ€™s office. Originally located at the site of the current Gateway Shopping Center on Donner Pass Road (historic US 40). It was donated to the Truckee Donner Historical Society in 1990 and moved to this location on May 23, 1991. Thanks to community efforts, the cabin has been restored and serves as a historical research library.

name: George Schaffer
description: One of Truckeeâ€™s earliest settlers, Schaffer in 1866 built the first lumber mill in the town. He freighted the locomotive 'San Mateo' to Truckee in a winter crossing of the Sierra prior to the laying of Central Pacific tracks. This building 'The Star Hotel' was built by Schaffer probably for his family. When mill operations were relocated up Martis Creek in 1871, this building was converted to a hotel and has remained in continuous operation to this day. For 24 years, the Truckee Basin supplied timber to railroads, mines and boom towns through 'Uncle George's' enterprises. Note: The Schaffer family cemetery, located at 10233 Schaffer Dr., may be visited by the public.

name: Gilmore Flying Field
description: Gilmore Flying Field. This windsock marks the site of one of the first flying fields in the United States. Here Lyman Gilmore, a contemporary of the Wright Brothers, pioneered in aviation by designing and building his own planes and gliders as early as 1898. Plaque on monument in front of school dedicated 1968 by Grand Lodge, Free and Accepted Masons: Lyman Gilmore, Jr. 1874-1951. Lyman Gilmore, Jr. established the first commercial airport on this site in 1907. To him and his memory the people of this community dedicate this school as the Lyman Gilmore School. Note: Gilmore did not use this field until the winter of 1911-12. As the Daily Transcript reported on January 29, 1912, "The principal place of business [of the Gilmore Airship Co.] was recently moved from Colfax to Grass Valley, and the meeting on Saturday evening was the first with the present board of directors." This pole was used originally at Gilmore Field, which Gilmore always claimed was the first commercial airport on the West Coast. The CETA mural on the north wall of the multipurpose building was painted in 1978.

name: Glasson House
description: This Victorian/Greek Revival house was built in 1866 for Miles P. O'Connor, who came to California in 1849 and to Nevada County in 1852. He served as justice of the peace for Little York township in 1852-53 and for Grass Valley 1862-69. He served one term in the state assembly and two in the state senate. He earned a fortune from the famous Idaho gold mine and was instrumental in creation of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. After selling this house to John Polglase in 1873, he moved to San Jose and funded various institutions, including a sanitarium later known as O'Connor Hospital. John Polglase, a hard-rock miner from Cornwall, came to Grass Valley in 1864. In 1875 Polglase bought an interest in John Glasson's grocery and provisions store while continuing to work underground in the Idaho mine. In 1882 he sold the house to his partner, and Glasson and his heirs owned it from then until 1973. John Glasson, like Polglase, came here in 1864 from England, and like O'Connor he played a prominent role in Nevada County history. He bought the local gas company in 1885-86 and in 1888 merged it with the Grass Valley Electric Light and Motor Co. He built the countyÃ

name: Glenbrook Basin
description: Early In the 1850's Glenbrook Basin first became known as the site of county fairs, horse racing and other special events. When Olympia Park, located at the south end of the basin, opened in 1901, Glenbrook Basin became the recreational center of Nevada County and much of Northern California, and remained so for over fifty years. People would come to the basin by horse and buggy or later the automobile, but those wishing for a little more excitement would often use the Nevada County Traction Companyâ€™s trolley, or the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. The large grandstands of the three-quarter-mile racetrack stood near the junction of present Brunswick Road and Sutton Way. Trotting, pacing, and running horse races were held at the track, and motor car races began in 1913. The racetrack was one of the first electrically lighted racetracks in the state. A dam was constructed just northeast of the existing electrical substation to form Lake Olympia. The lake soon became one of the BasinÃ

name: Gold Quartz Discovery
description: This tablet commemorates the discovery of gold-bearing quartz and the beginning of quartz mining in California. The discovery was made on Gold Hill by George McKnight October, 1850 The occurrence of gold-bearing quartz was undoubtedly noted here and elsewhere about the same time, or previously, but the above discovery created the great excitement that started the development of quartz mining into a great industry. The Gold Hill Mine from 1850 to 1857 is credited with a total production of $4,000,000.

name: Golden Era Lounge/309 Broad Street
description: Buildings on this site have housed bars and saloons since the Gold Rush era. Diana Saloon was opened in 1856. Stumpf's Miner's Hotel was established in 1863, Frank's Place (Eilerman) Saloon in 1887, and Costello & Jepson in 1910. The current building was constructed in 1886 following a major fire. W. B. Celio and H. L. Gove purchased the property in 1906, and it remained in the Celio family until 2015. Costello & Jepson established a "soda fountain" during the Prohibition years, followed by a series of bars that included the Success and Duffy's Success. Golden Era opened in 2015. The Brunswick-Balke-Collender Co.'s 'The Record' bar was built circa 1895. The front and back bars were installed in 1904 and 1915 respectively.

name: Graniteville
description: A camp established in this area in 1850 was called Eureka; then later, Eureka South. By 1856 it sometimes had a winter population of 1000. Rich placer mining deposits were played out by 1865, but quartz mining continued. By 1867, total gold taken from this area was estimated at 20 million dollars. Area also important as distribution point for water from reservoirs, fed to hydraulic mines below by ditches. Name changed to Graniteville when Post Office was established on Aug. 26, 1867.

name: Grass Valley AME Church and School Site
description: "Across the street, on lots 315, 317, 319 and 323 S. Church Street, the Grass Valley African Methodist Episcopal Church and School were erected in 1854 and 1865 respectively by the local African American community. Reverend Peter Green and Church trustee and former slave Isaac Sanks, along with the other members of the congregation, worked to abolish slavery, secure voting rights for African American men, eliminate the ban on testimony of African Americans in court, and gain access to public accommodations and education. The AME Church was known for its exceptional choir and accomplished musicians. It was the site for fundraising events, literary readings, and the celebration of the Emancipation Proclamation. African American parents, whose children were denied entry into white schools, built their own schoolhouse next to the Church. Both the Church and School were sold in 1893 at public auction to Charles Clinch and the funds returned to the AME Church. Shortly thereafter, the new owners built the four Victorian homes that still stand on the property today."

name: Grass Valley Hardware
description: Plaque dedicated 1969 by Native Daughters and Native Sons of the Golden West: Through the years of prospecting hydraulic and hardrock mining this hardware business, one of CaliforniaÃ

name: Grass Valley Historical District
description: Dedicated in 1969 by the Grass Valley City Planning Commission. Description: A colorful, old gold-mining town established in 1849 at Boston Ravine and later named Grass Valley by a company of emigrants. The atmosphere of early mining days still clings to the town. Significant sites include the Empire Mine State Historic Park, the North Star House and the homes of Lola Montez and Lotta Crabtree.

name: Grass Valley Masonic Lodge
description: Masonry in Grass Valley. Madison Lodge #23 was established under dispensation from the Grand Lodge of California on May 4th 1852. Its permanent charter was issued on May 5th 1853. Madison Lodge held its first two meetings at the old Columbus School on the present site of Hennessy School. The Lodge moved to a building on the south side of Main St.  where it now meets Auburn St.  This wooden building was destroyed along with most of Grass Valley in the great fire of September 12 and 13, 1855. A few days later, the Masons met with the Odd Fellows to select a site for a new brick building to house both organizations at 110 Mill St. This building was Madison's home for nearly 73 years, until fire destroyed the Masonic portion of the building in December 1928. For the next nine years the Lodge met in the auditorium of the old Strand Theater at 161 Mill St.  During this time, planning was being completed for our present building, which saw its first meeting on January 29, 1938. For 150-years, in both good times and bad, through 7 wars and a great depression, the Masonic spirit of Brotherly Love, Relief and Truth has continued on through our brethren of honorable men of all backgrounds, professions and faith. This plaque was set on May 9, 2002 in rededication ceremonies conducted by C. Ray Whitaker, Grand Master of Masons in California and Elmer B. Curtis, Master of Madison Lodge.

name: Grass Valley Public Library (Josiah Royce Library)
description: There are four plaques. First plaque and fir tree dedicated 1931 by Native Daughters and Native Sons of the Golden West: 1854 - Himalayan Fir - 1916. Planted In Memory of Dr. Josiah Royce one of AmericaÃ

name: Grass Valley's Famed Maple
description: This beautiful, Brandywine Maple, subject for artists and photographers, was brought from the fields of Gettysburg, and planted in 1876 by the widow of the poet and editor of the Grass Valley National newspaper, John Rollin Ridge. Surviving the fire which leveled the home, it now adorns the lovely Victorian home built by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Edward Clinch in 1900. Roy Clinch Tremoureux, grandson, notes proudly that many trees which beautify our town have come from this famed maple. Note: Time has ravaged the maple.

name: Hard Rock Gold Mining
description: Hard Rock Gold Mining 1850-1957. Within a one-mile radius of this spot more than 500 million dollars worth of gold was produced by deep mines, including the Empire, North Star and the Idaho-Maryland. During that time this was the worldÃ

name: Hartung House
description: In August of 1888 Nevada and Emma (Schwartz) Hartung purchased about 220 acres bordering Pleasant Valley Rd. and Piper Lane in Pleasant Valley (now Penn Valley). At the time of purchase, a small house and a barn were on the property. These two structures likely were built in the early 1860s when the property was owned by James and Hannah Saxon. The first house, still standing in the 1930â€™s, had deteriorated and was razed. The well, located in the front porch of the house remains. The barn now extended on both ends, still exists. The Hartungs built the current house in 1889. Pear, apple and walnut crops became the primary means of support for the family. A few of the original pear trees remain. Nevada August Hartung was born in August 1853 on the shores of Webber Lake, California in a wagon train on the family's way west from Illinois. It is reported that Nevada's given name was the result of the parentsâ€™ awe at their first sight of the Sierra Nevada Mountains. He grew up on his father Adolph Hartung's farm at Kentucky Flat and married Emma Schwartz in 1880. By the time of their 1888 purchase of the property, the Hartungs had three children, Frank (b.1880), Herman (b. 1884) and Mamie (b. 1886). The Hartungs bought the farm from Samuel and Agnes Yeo (owners 1888-1888) and prior to that the property was owned by Joseph Peers (1886-1888) and the Saxons (ca.1860-1886). In 1849-1850, John Rose and William Reynolds operated a trading post and cattle corral, both thought to be located on what became the Hartung property. The current owner, Pete Brennan, acquired the property in 1971 and operates a horse boarding facility.

name: Hirschman's Pond
description: "Nevada County's diverse gold rush population included a large Jewish community. Most were immigrants from Europe, attracted by the opportunity to make their fortunes and by the County's relative freedom and absence of discrimination against Jews. Many were merchants and many were miners. One of the more successful miners was Leb Hirschman (1823-1893), a native of Bavaria, who arrived here in the early 1850s, and mined around Oustamah Hill. In 1866, Hirschman, Grover & Co. purchased the American Hill Diggins, one of the the principal hydraulic mines in Nevada City, and operated it for many years. This pond, on the site of the mine, bears his name."

name: Hobart Mills Site
description: First plaque dedicated 1938 by descendants of Walter Scott Hobart is about 0.2 m. north of the junction of Hobart Mills Rd with Old Hwy 89 on west side of Old 89: This monument marks the townsite of Hobart Mills, California. A logging and lumbering enterprise of sixty-six years life in the Sierra Nevada mountains it commemorates and honors its founder Walter Scott Hobart 1839-1892 and those whose loyalty, industry and ability made its successful operation possible.

name: Hobart Mills Site Second Plaque
description: Walter Scott Hobart's Sierra Nevada Wood & Lumber Company moved machinery, buildings and railroad equipment here from Lake Tahoe in 1896. The town and business flourished for 40 years, ceasing operations in 1936. Fibreboard Corporation operated a mill and railroad here from 1946 to 1955, logging timber purchased from Southern Pacific. The old mill was burned in 1964. Hobart Mills was an important lumbering center and a major contributor to the heritage of the Eastern Sierra.

name: Holbrooke Hotel
description:
Holbrooke Hotel Founded 1851. Californiaâ€™s oldest hotel in continuous operation. Among its century-old archives are names of such notables as Presidents Grant, Harrison, Cleveland; authors Mark Twain, Bret Harte; boxers Corbett, Fitzsimmons; actor Gilbert Barry, and highwayman Black Bart. Present structure built in 1862 following infamous fire.

Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1978 by State of California, CHL 914: Ã±The present bar of the hotel has been in continuous operation since 1852, when it was known as the Golden Gate Saloon. The wooden building had a rear extension called The Exchange Hotel and offered food and lodging by January 1853. The saloon was destroyed in the fire of 1855 and rebuilt out of fieldstone with a brick front. The Holbrooke, built in 1862, had the adjacent Golden Gate Saloon incorporated in the hotel building.

Third plaque dedicated by Grass Valley Downtown Association: Presidential Square. These Presidents of the United States have visited this site: Ulysses S. Grant, James A. Garfield, Benjamin Harrison, Grover Cleveland, Herbert Hoover.  Note: No evidence exists that some of these notables stayed at the Holbrooke or visited Grass Valley.  Local lore has it that Gilbert Barry is a Clamper fabrication.

name: Hooligan Rock
description: Behind this broken rock, vigilante justice in the form of a tar and feather party or other form of physical punishment was meted out to the villains and ruffians who plagued Truckee through the dawn of the 20th-century. Constable Jacob Teeter bravely fought against vigilantism and his friend, attorney C. F. McGlashan, blasted the rock, ending its symbolism of terror, and used parts of the rock in a wall around his mansion. His granddaughter, Nona, recalled that the rock formed a 'backdrop of ugly secret scenes, floggings in the night, scalding tar and a smother of feathers, curses and screams for mercy.

name: Humbug/North Blomfield Cemetery
description: "This 'hallowed place', that resides with in the boundaries of the Malakoff State Historical Park represents a 'tapestry of humanity.'  Those who dared to carve out a place for themselves in an unknown and untamed wilderness. They came seeking riches, changing the landscape they encountered for all of time. They left behind a legacy entwined with turmoil, destruction, invention and lives lived hard. In 1855, Isreal Joiner, who was shot for stealing gold from a 'sluice box', and George Carter who lived to be 80, were the first to be buried in Humbug Cemetery. In 1857 when the town got its post office, the name was changed to North Bloomfield. George Edwards, John Enders, John McNulty, and Louise Miller; they were the first to be buried under the new banner, North Bloomfield Cemetery. By the end of the Nineteenth Century, as the mining played out, this town was all but dead. However, in an almost bazaar [sic] manner this cemetery has continued to grow!  As of 1984 there were 170 recorded buried here from Austria, Australia, Bavaria, Canada, China, Denmark, England, France, Germany, Holland, Ireland, Portugal, Prussia, Scotland and America. In March 2017, Debra Phanner, a long time Park asst. was buried here. So, as you enter this portal into the past know that you are walking on 'hallowed ground' with many untold stories waiting to be discovered!"

name: Hunt's Hill (Gouge Eye)
description: Hunt's Hill (also Gouge Eye) is a former mining camp founded in 1852 by a miner named Hunt. It was located on one of the deepest parts of the rich Blue Lead channel of gold-bearing gravel. In 1855, one of the mining claims established by some French miners, was â€œjumped.â€

name: Hydraulic Gate Valve
description: Hydraulic gold mining 21-inch gate valve 1880 Used at the You Bet-Red Dog Diggin's east of Nevada City periodically 1880- 1935 to control water flow from Chalk Bluff reservoir to the mine. Hydraulic nozzle mounted opposite was operated at that mine by Thomas B. Brady. Manufactured by Joshua Handy Works, San Francisco. Wt. 1,500 lbs. approx.  A gift from the Brady Family.

name: Hydraulic Mining
description: The first hydraulic mining originated here at American Hill Diggins by Edward Matteson in 1853. Matteson originally used canvas hose. The canvas hose was replaced with crinoline hose in 1860. These miners' giants, also known as water cannons, monitors and dictators, washed hillsides away with high pressure water, uncovering gold bearing beds. This mining technique flourished profitably until 1884. Rivers were being filled with mud and debris causing flooding of the rich farm lands in the valley. At that time, a lawsuit brought by farmers against hydraulic mining was heard in Marysville, California where a decision known as The Sawyer Decision stopped all mining. Under a special use permit, granted by the Department of Fish and Game, these hydraulic monitors were last used at the Pacific Slab Mine near Foresthill, California in 1970, which was documented by the California Department of Parks and Recreation.

name: Idaho Maryland Core Sample
description: Plaque dedicated 1961 by Historical Society: Core - Weight 11 Tons. Portion taken from Idaho Shaft No. 2 bored to a vertical depth of 1,125 feet, diameter of shaft five feet, the core boring barrel was invention of J. B. Newsom, mining engineer. Was used as an experiment by the Idaho Maryland Mine Corporation under general manager Errol MacBoyle. With the cooperation of the directors of the 17th District Agricultural Association, through their manager, Malcolm E. Hammill, this core was erected in July 1961. Note: The Newsom drilling machine bored a round shaft, using no explosives. It was cheaper because no timbering was needed. Similar cores are displayed at Calanan Park and Empire Mine Park.

name: Idaho-Maryland Mine Site
description: The Idaho quartz mine was discovered in 1863. It prospered under the Coleman brothers from 1867-93, then consolidated with the Maryland and other small mines. Eventually it became one of the 'Big Three' of the Northern Mines, the other two being the Empire and the North Star. Its final years under the management of Errol MacBoyle were very productive. He developed a revolutionary method of shaft sinking, using a huge core drill called the Newsom drilling machine. World War II and government order L208 brought about final closing. Note: Various parts of the mine still exist. The office building is off the east side of Centennial Drive, between Idaho Maryland Road and Whispering Pines Lane. A headframe is located near the Grass Valley Scrap, Iron & Metal yard. Learn more about the mine from Gold in Quartz: The Legendary Idaho Maryland Mine by Jack Clark, or from MacBoyle's Gold by Gage McKinney, both available from Comstock Bonanza Press.

name: Indian Grinding Rock
description: Fifth plaque: Indian Grinding Rock. Indian women used grinding rocks such as these, along with a linear rock called a Ã¯pestleÃ

name: Indian Medicine Stone
description: Pioneer Emigrant Trail. This boulder was known to the pioneers as the Indian Medicine Stone. On its top are hollows in which the Indians lay while taking sun baths to cure their ills. One branch of the Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County and to the Valley of the Sacramento passed close beside this stone.

name: Isabel Hefelfinger Wagon Shed
description:
The Wagon Shed. Generations of Californians living in the northern mines depended for their economics and social well-being on wheel transportation powered by the horse, mule, the ox. The vehicles displayed here represent some of those used to move people, goods and gold. The Nevada County Historical Society, Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, City of Nevada City, California Department of Parks & Recreation, Nevada City Lions Club and many other civic groups and individuals cooperated to preserve this collection.

Second plaque dedicated 1993 by the Historical Society: Isabel Hefelfinger Wagon Shed. This building is dedicated to the memory of Isabel Ross Hefelfinger (1899-1991). She encouraged many by her interest and dedication in remembering the past. Her untiring efforts to preserve history of Nevada County will not be forgotten.

name: Jacob van Blaren
description: Anthony House Way Station, Pub & Tavern was located just off shore from Commodore Park. It was a stage stop between Marysville & the Virginia City Silver Mines. Jacob Van Blaren, a Civil War bugler, stopped at Anthony House for a period of time and died while staying there. He was in his forties at his death. Proprietors of Anthony House decided to bury him behind the building and it wasn't until Boise Cascade was in negotiations with the historical society that they named the area surrounding his burial site Bugler Park. It is uncertain at what point in Lake Wildwood's history that the park's name was changed to Commodore Park

name: Jax Truckee Diner
description: Originally built in October 1948 by Kullman Diner Co. Newark, New Jersey and shipped to West Chester, PA. known as the “Birmingham Grill” where it continually operated until purchased by the Carey Family in 1992. On July 26, 1995 the diner arrived in Truckee, California. No other diner of it's (sic) size and class had ever been trucked across the continent. After extensive restoration the diner was re-opened to the public on December 7, 1995.

name: Jibboom Street
description: Wooden plaque dedicated and placed in Pastime Club Bar 1969. Bronze plaque rededicated and placed in present location 1979  by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus: Jibboom Street. Sierra Site of the Worldâ€™s Oldest Profession 1867-1949.The stone and bronze plaque have been removed and are awaiting rededication

name: Kentucky Flat School
description: he Kentucky Flat School was originally located near the Kentucky Flat mining camp below Kentucky Ridge along the old road to Bridgeport, today's Bitney Springs Road. Within a few years the mining camp's gold had been depleted and its residents moved on. In 1855, the Kentucky Flat School was moved to its present location at Newtown, two miles east of Kentucky Flat. The towns were two of the earliest established gold mining camps in Nevada County, both founded in 1850. Mr. Hubertus Schardin, who emigrated from Germany, named Newtown and is considered its founder. He established a store and market there in 1850 to supply the miners. On August 21, 1877 Mr. Schardin deeded to the Kentucky Flat School District the property the school now sits on. Tragically, in 1895 the original Kentucky Flat School was destroyed by fire. It was quickly rebuilt the same year. The School continued to operate for 108 years, until 1963 when it was closed after proudly serving generations of Nevada County school children. The School was annexed to the Grass Valley School District in July 1964 and leased to the Kentucky Flat Community Center. Today, in 2022 it continues to serve young children from surrounding areas as the Kentucky Flat Community Pre-school

name: Kentucky Ridge Mine Site
description: During gold rush days, Nevada County had a significant Black population. Most were free, but even though California had entered the Union as a free state, some were enslaved before the Civil War. From 1851-1852, the Kentucky Ridge Mine was the largest enterprise in California operated by slave labor. Gold was first discovered on this property in early 1851. By October of 1851, Colonel William F. English brought with him from Florida and South Carolina about 40 slaves to operate the gold mine. He was one of over a dozen slave owners in Nevada County. The mine failed after English died in a gun accident on August 27, 1852, and the Black mine workers gradually moved into the towns.
